title: Here's Luck
content:
آ آ آ آ Old Time is tramping close to-day, you hear his bluchers fall,
آ آ آ آ A mighty change is on the way, anï؟½ God protect us all;
آ آ آ آ Some dustï؟½ll fly from beery coats, at least itï؟½s been declared.
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½m glad that wimin has the votes, but just a trifle scared.

آ آ آ آ Iï؟½m just a trifle scared, For why? The wimin mean to rule;
آ آ آ آ It makes me feel like days gone by when I was caned at school.
آ آ آ آ The days of men is nearly dead, of double moons and stars, 
آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½ll soon put out our pipes, ï؟½tis said, anï؟½ close the public bars.

آ آ آ آ No more weï؟½ll take a glass of ale when pushed with care anï؟½ strife,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½chuckle home with that old tale we used to tell the wife.
آ آ آ آ Weï؟½ll laugh anï؟½joke anï؟½ sing no more with jolly beery chums,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ shout ï؟½Hereï؟½s luck!ï؟½ while waitinï؟½ for the luck that never comes.

آ آ آ آ Did we prohibit swillinï؟½ tea clean out of common-sense
آ آ آ آ Or legislate on gossipinï؟½ across a backyard fence?
آ آ آ آ Did we prohibit bustles, or the hoops when they was here?
آ آ آ آ The wimin never think of this, they want to stop our beer.

آ آ آ آ The track oï؟½ life is dry enough, anï؟½ crossed with many a rut,
آ آ آ آ But, oh! weï؟½ll find it long anï؟½ rough when all the pubs is shut,
آ آ آ آ When all the pubs is shut, anï؟½ gone the doors we used to seek,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ we go toilinï؟½, thirstinï؟½ on through Sundays all the week.

آ آ آ آ For since the days when pubs was ï؟½innsï؟½, in years gone pastï؟½nï؟½ far, 
آ آ آ آ Poor sinful souls have drowned their sins anï؟½ sorrers at the bar;
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ though at times it led to crimes, anï؟½ debt, and such complaints, 
آ آ آ آ I scarce dare think about the time when all mankind is saints.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twould make the bones of Bacchus leap anï؟½ break his coffin lid;
آ آ آ آ And Burnsï؟½s ghost would wail anï؟½ weep as Bobby never did.
آ آ آ آ But let the preachers preach in style, anï؟½ rave and rant, ï؟½nï؟½ buck,
آ آ آ آ I rather guess theyï؟½ll hear awhile the old war-cry: ï؟½Hereï؟½s Luck!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ The world might wobble round the sun, anï؟½ all the banks go bung,
آ آ آ آ But pipesï؟½ll smoke anï؟½ liquor run while Auld Lang Syne is sung.
آ آ آ آ While men are driven through the mill, anï؟½ flinty times is struck,
آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½ll find a private entrance still! Hereï؟½s Luck, old man, Hereï؟½s Luck!
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Lawson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Henry Lawson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
